title: The No-Commitment (Open) Mortgage
metaDescription: Fully open mortgages let you pay off the balance any time without penalty. Useful for short hold periods, pending sales and bridge situations.
intro: An open mortgage can be paid off at any time — in part or in full — without any prepayment penalty. The trade-off: a higher rate. Useful when you genuinely don't know your timeline, or you're holding the property for less than 12 months.

body.heading: When an open mortgage makes sense
paragraphs: If you're going to sell within a year (downsizing, relocating, separation), the cost of a closed-mortgage early-payout penalty can vastly exceed the rate premium of an open product. Open mortgages typically run 1–2.5% above closed equivalent rates — significant on the carrying cost, but trivial compared to a $20,000+ IRD penalty.

Other use cases: pending estate sales, short-term holds during construction or renovation projects, properties you're going to refinance imminently for unrelated reasons, or any situation where flexibility is worth more than the rate.

whoItsFor: Plan to sell or refinance the property within 12 months
Are in an estate, separation or transitional situation

faqs.question: How much higher is the open rate?
faqs.answer: Typically 1–2.5% above the equivalent closed term. On a $700,000 mortgage that's $7,000–$17,500 per year of extra interest — but if you're holding under a year, this can still be cheaper than a closed-mortgage IRD.

faqs.question: Can I switch from open to closed later?
faqs.answer: Yes — open mortgages can be converted to closed terms at any time, at the prevailing closed rate, with no penalty. Many clients use an open product as a 'wait and see' before locking long-term.
